calendar.matters:
DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-09-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: CROSS DEFENDANT MARIA BAEZA VARELA's, AN INDIVIDUAL, DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: The unopposed demurrer to the second cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty in the second amended cross-complaint is sustained with leave to amend. "Before a person can be charged with a fiduciary obligation, he must either knowingly undertake to act on behalf and for the benefit of another, or must enter into a relationship which imposes that undertaking as a matter of law." (City of Hope National Medical Center v. Genentech, Inc. (2008) 43 Cal.4th 375, 386 [citation simplified].) The role of part-time bookkeeper is not a fiduciary relationship as a matter of law. The cross-complaint does not allege facts that the cross-defendant agreed to be a fiduciary; at most it discloses that the cross-complainant shared sensitive information with her. Because the court has no information about what facts, if any, cross-complainant could plead to show that there was agreement to fiduciary obligations, the court grants leave to amend.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)

DEFENDANT RALPH OSTERHOUT'S MOTION TO CHANGE OF VENUE (TENTATIVE RULING PURPOSES ONLY PART 2 OF 2)
Off Calendar Feb-11-2025 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Change Of Venue
Off Calendar Feb-11-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT RALPH OSTERHOUT's Motion For Change Of Venue.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Ralph Osterhout's motion to transfer venue is denied.

First, Osterhout has not shown venue is improper in San Francisco, such that the court must transfer the action to Sonoma County per CCP 396(b). Venue here is proper because plaintiff Maria Varela allegedly entered her employment contract in San Francisco and experienced labor violations here as well. (Varela decl. para. 7; Govt. Code 12965(c)(3) ["A [FEHA] action may be brought in any county in the state in which the unlawful practice is alleged to have been committed"].)

Second, Osterhout has not shown a discretionary transfer is warranted. If an action is filed in the proper county, the court has discretion to transfer venue "[w]hen the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the change." (CCP 397(c).) Osterhout's showing is a general one: he submits a declaration that he is infirm, but no other evidence. His briefing makes general assertions about other witnesses and their convenience but he offers no evidentiary showing as to other witnesses.

"Generally, the 'convenience of the parties is not to be considered upon a motion for a change of venue.' ' (Rycz v. Superior Court of San Francisco County (2022) 81 Cal.App.5th 824, 836 [Rycz] [quoting Peiser v. Mettler (1958) 50 Cal.2d 594, 612].) Instead, the court considers convenience of nonparty witnesses, but not those who are employees of the litigants. (Weil & Brown, Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2024) para. 3:555, 3:558.) "Before the convenience of witnesses may be considered as a ground for an order granting a change of venue it must be shown that their proposed testimony is admissible, relevant and material to some issue in the case as shown by the record before the court. The declaration or declarations supporting the motion should set forth the names of the witnesses, the nature of the testimony expected from each, and the reasons why the attendance of each would be inconvenient." (Rycz, supra, 81 Cal.App.5th at p. 836 [internation quotations and citations omitted].)

Here, Osterhout fails to meet his burden of showing transfer to Sonoma County would be most convenient for witnesses. Although the Osterhout declaration suggests it would be burdensome for him to litigate this action in San Francisco, the burdens to a party are not considered. The Osterhout declaration does not identify any specific nonparty witnesses, let alone set forth their proposed testimony or explain why attendance for each in San Francisco would be inconvenient. (Rycz, supra, 81 Cal.App.5th 824, 836.)

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
